data.title: IXN: All You Need To Know About This Global Tech ETF
data.description:
Introduction to the iShares Global Tech ETF

The iShares Global Tech ETF (IXN) is a $7.5B-sized financial product (by assets under management) that is issued by Blackrock, Inc. under their ‘iShares’ brand of ETFs. IXN, which initially got listed in November 2001, is priced at an expense ratio of 0.39%, and offers an annualized distribution yield of less than 1% (distributions are made twice a year).

What Does IXN Do?

IXN is a passively managed ETF that attempts to track the performance of an index crafted by another independent party (S&P Global) called the S&P Global 1200 Information Technology 4.5/22.5/45 Capped Index [SG1IT]. SG1IT, in turn, is a subset of the S&P Global 1200 index [SG1], and seeks to pick out stocks from the latter which are defined as “information technology”. Note that SG1 captures the top 70% of the overall global market cap (making it tilt mainly to giant and large-caps, while it also weights its constituents on the basis of their free float market-cap) and picks up stocks from specific regions such as the US (represented by the S&P 500), Europe (S&P Europe 350), Japan (S&P TOPIX 150), Canada (S&P TSX 60), Australia (S&P/ASX All Australian 50), Asia (S&P Asia 50), and Latin America (S&P Latin America 40). Based on the indices mentioned above, it looks like SG1IT attempts to source tech stocks from all global regions except Africa, and the Middle East.

SG1IT, which rebalances its constituents four times per year, also seeks to impose certain caps on individual holdings to ameliorate concentration effects. For instance, during each quarterly rebalance, no single stock is permitted to account for more than 22.5% of the entire index, and all stocks with individual weights of over 4.5% are not permitted to “jointly aggregate” to 45% of the total index.

It appears that IXN, which covers 127 stocks in total, does an acceptable job of tracking its target index, as its tracking errors across different time periods are in line with the median ETF levels.

What Are the Dominant Traits of IXN's Portfolio?

IXN may have the word “global” in its nomenclature, and while it certainly does dabble with stocks from at least six different foreign countries across the world, at the end of the day, this is still very much a US-centric product, with 75% of the portfolio domiciled domestically.

Then, IXN covers businesses from across the technology spectrum, but broadly they can be bracketed into three buckets:

  • Semiconductors and manufacturers of semiconductor equipment
  • Technology hardware businesses
  • Software & services

Of these categories, nearly half the portfolio comes from the first bucket.

Since IXN’s tracking index is a float-adjusted market-cap weighted index (put simply, stocks with a higher market-cap receive a higher weight in the index), there’s little surprise to note that this is a product that is overwhelmingly dominated by giant and large-caps which account for over 90% of the portfolio. Meanwhile, the average market-cap of over half a trillion dollars only reiterates the strong giant-cap positioning of IXN.

Then, even though IXN covers 127 global tech stocks and seeks to impose certain caps on its individual holdings, that doesn’t take away from the fact that this is a top-heavy portfolio with just the top 5 (or the top 4% of the holdings by number)- Nvidia (NVDA), Apple Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) jointly accounting for a mammoth 43% of the entire portfolio by value.

Stylistically, over half of this portfolio consists of growth-style stocks (stocks characterized by strong sales and earnings growth, price momentum over months, those that reinvest excess cash into the business rather than reward shareholders by way of dividends or share repurchases, etc.), although there’s also a useful component (over one-third of the portfolio) of blended stocks which don’t just have the typical growth characteristics, but also some value traits such as relatively cheap valuation multiples, strong dividend payouts, etc.

What Are The Risks Associated With IXN?

Investors who like to see steady consistent returns from their portfolio holdings, may not take comfort from the erratic nature of IXN’s return profile. To elaborate, while your typical ETF tends to see annualized volatility of 13% in a year, IXN’s volatility profile is considerably higher at over 21%.

For further insight, take a look at the image below which shows how divergent IXN’s tracking index's annual returns have been over the last decade. Even over a span of just two years (2022/2023), we’ve seen wild swings from a -30% drawdown in the first year, to a +54% profit in the following year!

Also take note of IXN’s relatively high sensitivity to the movements of the S&P 500. Over the past three years, IXN’s beta over the prime equity benchmark has worked out to 1.22x, so this tells us that for every 1% movement in the latter, the former can be expected to move by 1.22%. This could prove to be lucrative during a bull run, but the reverse is true during a bear market, making IXN something of a concern, when risk aversion in the markets are on the wane.

Given the caps that IXN seeks to put in place every quarter, and given the strong interest for US tech mega caps (which could end up expanding their market-caps and relative exposure within IXN’s portfolio), this is an ETF that is going to be susceptible to plenty of transaction activity, which won’t be particularly efficient, if you own it in a taxable brokerage account. To provide further context, while the median annual turnover ratio for an ETF is less than 30%, IXN’s annual turnover ratio is 1.5x more at 45%, and the constant rebalancing is likely to ramp the transaction costs which could also end up eating into the returns.

Technology stocks are typically not known for being generous with their distributions to shareholders, and investors of IXN should not be under the impression of assuming that this ETF (which focuses on these tech stocks) is embarking on a new path of distribution largesse. To shed more clarity, note that in December last year, IXN’s total distributions (recall, it makes these distributions twice a year) spiked by a massive 360% YoY at $0.93 per share. However, if investors breakdown that distribution, they will note that it was not because IXN’s holdings decided to distribute higher dividends (in fact, relative to December 2024, the income component actually contracted by -13%), but rather because they saw a substantial capital gain of $0.75 per share. Since, IXN tracks an index that imposes caps on its holdings, it will be forced to sell its holdings once those caps are breached resulting in a long-term capital gain. All, in all, if one just looks at the annualized dividend income (excluding capital gains) facet of IXN, which is more representative of its dividend profile, it only equates to a yield of less than 0.3% (well below the median ETF yield of 2.74%).

Investors who are mindful of themes of mean reversion in the financial markets, may be troubled to note that global “tech” stocks, look like one of the most overextended counters within global equity markets (the current relative strength ratio of IXN to a more diversified basket of global stocks is more than double its long-term mean of 0.39x and may be vulnerable to some downward mean reversion pressure going forward.

Who Is IXN For?

Since IXN is still largely dominated by US-domiciled technology stocks, it’s fair to say that this product would be suitable for those who appreciate a strong tilt towards the US technology bucket (which is still largely seen as the hub of the global innovation), without getting entirely wedged to that pocket (25% of IXN’s portfolio consists of global technology stocks which offers some element of diversification). These investors also recognize that the technology supply chain, particularly with the rapid penetration of artificial intelligence is no longer primarily centered in the US, and is also gradually picking up steam in other regions across the world, and want to also gradually profit from that movement.

Investors who want a pool of high growth stocks that are not necessarily the priciest from a valuation angle (more commonly known as GARP-style stocks, or those offering growth-at-a-reasonable-price) will likely also fancy a portfolio like IXN. Compared to its sister product- the iShares US Technology ETF (IYW), which covers 139 domestic technology stocks or the most popular growth ETF- the Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG), IXN’s holdings are poised to deliver the most favorable long-term earnings growth (19%). Higher growth usually translates to higher valuation multiples, but in this case, IXN is the cheapest out of the lot, priced at an earnings multiple of 18x which implies a 15% discount over US tech, and a 30% discount over US growth.

IXN will also appeal to investors who like cost-efficient ETFs; despite having to fish for stocks from across the world, which is not particularly straightforward and easy to procure, IXN is priced at a reasonable expense ratio of only 0.39%, which is around 11bps cheaper than the median ETF expense rate of 0.5%.

Peers of IXN

Besides IXN, two other ETF alternatives focusing on the global tech landscape are the iShares Future Exponential Technologies ETF (XT), and the The SP Funds S&P Global Technology ETF (SPTE).

XT is also issued by Blackrock and looks like a more futuristic product as it covers both developed and EM businesses involved in the development of exponential technologies that will likely displace older technologies (the coverage of XT is wider than IXN, but its average market-cap suggests that it is a mega-cap focused fund). Like IXN, XT while global in scope, still mainly tilts to the US (69% exposure), and is also prone to the same degree of churn. XT also offers a stellar trailing yield, but like IXN the most recent distribution isn’t driven by income, but by capital gains (in XT’s case, it isn’t just long-term capital gains, but short-term capital gains as well.

SPTE, which is relatively new (set-up only in 2023), and issued by Shariah funds, tracks a global tech index (only half the portfolio is US-based, offering solid diversification) that is Shariah-compliant (composed in accordance with Islamic law). SPTE, which doesn’t witness as much of churn as the other two, also offers the useful feature of monthly distributions, however when those distributions are annualized, it isn’t too compelling. Note however that SPTE is relatively more expensive to own, suffers from weak daily liquidity, offers the lowest coverage, and is heavily concentrated in its top 10 holdings.

Summary

IXN, covers 127 technology stocks, with a lion’s share of the portfolio based in the US. This product comes across as reasonably priced (both from an expense ratio angle, as well as the valuations of its holdings), offering solid earnings growth potential. Investors should be mindful of IXN’s relative high volatility, turnover, and heightened sensitivity to the key US equity benchmark.

This article answers three main questions about IXN:

  1. What are the key features of IXN's portfolio?
  2. What type of investor is IXN suitable for, and what are the risks associated with it?
  3. What are the global tech ETF alternatives to IXN?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Nebius: Real Contracts, Real Economics, Real Upside
data.description:
Investment Summary

I rate Nebius (NBIS) as a Buy with the price target of $220, or about 41%of the upside of the current price at $156.14.

It is a simple thesis: Nebius is among the only slightly few actual AI cloud firms that have translated the AI infrastructure demand narrative into signed deals with the biggest technological firms in the world, and I believe that the market is still pricing it as though that evidence has not yet entirely come in.

The demand is already there. Microsoft (MSFT)has invested between $17.4 and $19.4 billion, Meta (META) has signed a new deal worth up to $27 billion, and NVIDIA (NVDA) invested directly into the company to the tune of $2 billion and formalized a partnership to deploy over 5 gigawatts of NVIDIA systems by 2030.

The valuation has a clear path. I value Nebius on the 2026 exit ARR guidance of $7 billion to $9 billion, suppose $8.5 billion on the upper-end, and multiply it by 7x to come up with the approximate value of $235 per share, but bring it down to $220 by a modest 6 percent discount of dilution risk and execution risk.

The unit economics already work. Currently, prior to the buildout even being completed, the Q4 2025 release has a core AI cloud producing a revenue of $214.2 million with a 24% adjusted EBITDA margin. The question of profitability is no longer a hypothetical one, and that makes the nature of the bet significant.

What Nebius Actually Does

Nebius is literally becoming an "all-in-one AI development store", a cloud platform that does all the work, all the way down to the bottom (raw computing power) to deploying AI models into production.

Its flagship product, Nebius AI Cloud, provides the AI teams with the resources they really require to execute heavy workloads, like GPU clusters, high-speed networking, scalable storage, and orchestration software, such as Kubernetes and Slurm.

The interesting part is that instead of being a generic cloud provider that is attempting to offer services to all industries, Nebius is a laser-focused AI infrastructure provider, which may give it a real advantage with the sustained increase in the demand for purpose-built AI compute.

Nebius has structured its offerings on top of that infrastructure into a series of solutions that focus on particular workflows and industries. On the developer and enterprise fronts, they consist of model training, ML inference, model fine-tuning, data preparation, retrieval-augmented generation [RAG], and generative AI services.

In case of specific verticals, Nebius has developed life sciences and healthcare, media and entertainment, physical AI and robotics dedicated solutions. The latest one is Tavily by Nebius which bridges large language models and AI agents to a real-time web search via a single API, in agentic AI use cases. Collectively these solutions are relevant to the thesis since they will catapult Nebius up the value stack: a customer who trains, fine-tunes, inferences, and agentic searches on Nebius will be much harder to displace than a customer just renting GPUs by the hour.

Another service is Token Factory which is the managed production AI workload inference platform by Nebius. It provides dedicated endpoints, autoscaling throughput, and sub-second latency on open-source models, indicating that Nebius is selling a completed production service, rather than raw compute.

Pure infrastructure can be simply commodified on price, whereas a managed layer of production with low latency and zero operational overhead to the customer is significantly sticker. Nebius also integrates Avride, its AV and delivery robot platform, and TripleTen, an edtech reskilling business, as well as equity partners in other companies, such as ClickHouse and Toloka, beyond the core AI cloud. However, the hierarchy is evident in the Q4 earnings release where core AI cloud brought in $214.2 million in Q4, approximately 94% of group revenue, whereas TripleTen and Avride were approximately 6 percent and immaterial respectively.

Why Nebius Is More Than a GPU Rental Story

In March, Nebius released AI Cloud 3.5, including serverless AI, a data transfer service, and NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition. It also made an announcement of an agreement to acquire Tavily around the same time, introducing real-time search infrastructure into the Nebius platform, specifically in the use cases of agentic AI.

That is important to the bull case, as the greater Nebius can assist customers to develop complete AI systems rather than rent them compute; the more difficult it becomes to price Nebius like a commodity, the more justifiable the revenue is over time.

The move of Nebius to the second category is being driven by serverless tools, managed inference endpoints, agentic search using Tavily, and dedicated solutions. Suppose the given trajectory is realized; the multiple of ARR that I applied to my valuation is not only defensible; it begins to appear to be money on the table (I'll discuss this in the valuation section, but a 7x ARR multiple for Nebius is a conservative number considering the contracts and investments the company has already secured and the nature of increased AI spending in the global market).

I cannot 100% claim that Nebius has quite earned that platform premium yet, but the product roadmap shows that the company is purposefully heading in its direction and not falling into it, which is an important consideration when you are deciding whether the present price is a price of entry or that the company is on a downward spiral.

Capacity And Capital Are Finally Lining Up

The other point making me more constructive on the business is that, Nebius is doing a much better job of integrating demand, capacity, and funding simultaneously, and that case has been made much easier to argue by the last few months.

In March 2026, NVIDIA announced a $2 billion investment in Nebius and said the partnership is designed to help Nebius deploy more than 5 gigawatts of NVIDIA systems by the end of 2030. Around the same time, Nebius secured approval for a gigawatt-scale AI factory in Independence, Missouri, with a potential capacity of up to 1.2 GW, and then announced a new 310 MW AI factory in Lappeenranta, Finland.

It is not scaling or a company putting a server room here and there. This is Nebius actively creating a purposeful, coordinated push to create an actual global AI infrastructure presence, and the rate of site announcements in the past two quarters indicates the management takes the present demand window as a now-or-never opportunity.

In March 2026, Nebius closed about $4.3 billion of new convertible notes, in addition to the NVIDIA investment, and thus the company went into the critical buildout period with a war chest instead of a wish list. I hate dilution, and added leverage upon principle, but in this instance I believe the other option/alternative would have been much worse. A company that provides AI infrastructure cannot afford to take advantage of a in-a-decade demand spurt when it is financially shy about acquiring the inputs. I would prefer to have a company that pushes aggressively to seize power, hardware, and locations at the right time than one that fails to make the window at all due to an attempt to protect a nicer balance sheet.

Valuation

I believe that a somewhat realistic assumption/guesstimate would be about $220 per share, or approximately 41% higher than the current price of the stock of Nebius, which is $156.14 . The method I would arrive at is by appreciating Nebius on valuation on the 2026 exit ARR rather than a DCF and not on 2025 revenue. Management left 2025 with a figure of $1.25 billion ARR and they are aiming to have $7 to $9 billion ARR by 2026.

Assuming that Nebius achieves approximately $8.5 billion of exit ARR (whihc is an assumption I'm making towards the upper end of their guidance) and a 7x ARR multiple, I would come up with an implied value of approximately $59.5 billion.

Divide that by the 253.0 million shares outstanding at end-2025, and you will arrive at a figure of about $235/share before any haircut, which translates to $220, which means it'll only require a 6% discount of dilution and execution conservatism.

What is the logic behind that multiple? Since Nebius is no longer merely a "potentially someday GPU renter", I believe the market is not fully appreciating the change. It has already entered into a Microsoft contract between $17.4 billion and $19.4 billion, and in March, it declared a new Meta contract where it will deliver $12 billion of dedicated capacity, with Meta having an alternative to allocate more capacity should it not be sold to other companies at $15 billion.

The two of those relationships can be viewed as a possible revenue pipeline, which is bigger than anything that Nebius has billed to date, and the fact that two of the most demanding infrastructure purchasers in the world decided on Nebius instead of well-known alternatives is something that says a lot and kind of tells us that Nebius sits in the big leages. And I honestly think that a 7x ARR multiple is not even the highest of the high multiples that Nebius can achieve; I'd say it's arguably conservative.

These are not fundamentally mature, business-like cloud vendors being traded at 3x to 5x revenue; it is more of an early-stage platform infrastructure business, and they historically have traded at significantly higher multiples than I am valuing Nebius today. The nearest publicly-traded analog is CoreWeave (CRWV), which has been trading at much higher ARR multiples since its IPO since the market believes in the compounding effect of long-term contracted capacity, and Nebius shares the same structural properties: hyperscaler-grade users, contractually-committed demand, and a full-stack offering, which is meaningfully more difficult to imitate than raw GPU rental. My reason is that I am making 7x not because I think that is the ceiling but because that is the floor.

All it takes in the business to do this is very easy. I believe Nebius should convert signed demand into energized capacity (I believe timing is extremely crucial here), maintain a high utilization rate after the clusters have been installed, and demonstrate that the economy seen in late 2025 can be scaled in 2026 and in the next few years.

I'm somewhat confident that this is at least possible: Nebius generated $529.8 million of 2025 revenue, including $227.7 million in Q4, while the core AI cloud business produced $214.2 million of Q4 revenue and $51.8 million of adjusted EBITDA, a 24% margin on a cost base that included $2.1 billion of Q4 capex.

I think that when Nebius makes it anywhere within the top half of its 2026 ARR target, the stock will not be around the Street average target of about $170, it will be around and above a multiple of that number because the company will be demonstrating that it is not just a participant in AI infrastructure demand, but is one of the few scaled winners in the category and needs to be valued accordingly.

Risks

I base my whole valuation on the fact that Nebius will leave 2026 with $8.5 billion of ARR, which will need numerous big data center locations to be online in different countries. When one of the big locations loses a quarter, it does not simply harm reported revenue at that point; it drags an important portion out of the end of year run rate upon which the price target of $220. The customer base is already in place, and the contracts are already in place; it is not a question of whether the customers want what Nebius is constructing or not. It is simply a matter of whether Nebius can get it up quickly, and that is a more difficult thing to handicap on the outside than a revenue forecast.

The second risk is the extent of cash this business burns before it becomes self-funding. Nebius incurred about $2.1 billion in Q4 capex alone and has forecasted up to $5 billion in the entire year, all of which will go to the door before the revenue is realized. That is true only in the event that the capital markets remain open and ready to finance the gap. Assuming investor confidence in AI infrastructure reduces prior to Nebius growing large enough to produce sufficient cash to finance its own development, the business will probably require additional capital, and every new issue of equity dilutes the value per share that I have outlined above.

The third risk is one that lacks sufficient coverage: Nebius in its 2024 annual report reported material weaknesses in its internal controls, namely those related to IT systems, revenue recognition, and its fixed assets accounting.

I do not believe that it nullifies the investment case, and the management claims that it is rectifying them. But in a company that is expanding so fast, and raising so much money, poor financial controls are not a housekeeping problem; they are a fact of doubt about whether the figures that investors are basing their decisions on are as clean as they must be.

And look, I'm not an auditor, but I know a thing or two whenever management says something like this. It's usually included in the auditor's report, and the fact is, if they added this in the notes of the financial statements, then there's a chance for the company's financials to be misstated. Again, it won't hurt the thesis, but if it turns out the company's financials are materially misstated (meaning financials differ per audit and per books substantially)? That's a different story because then we'd question the reliability, accuracy, and completeness of audit assertions, but for now? Assumption is the FS is fairly represented.

Conclusion

I keep it simple: Nebius is no longer a tale, which you have to fantasize. It has picked up tens of billions of dollars of demand at Microsoft and Meta, a formalized NVIDIA partnership guaranteeing both equipment supply and strategic credibility, and a product stack that has left raw compute rentals far behind.

The Q4 shareholder letter reaffirms that the unit economics are already viable at the current size. And that is the most appealing part to me, as it implies that the question of profitability is no longer an abstraction.

These risks exist, and I have been frank on them: high Capex, complexity in the execution of different geographies, and internal controls that are yet to be sorted out (again, it may come off as subtle, but it'll be a big issue if they find out that their financials aren't reliable, but judging the investments that they have, they probably have done their due diligence to be confident investing in Nebius).

But those are more visible, risks that are well known to the market. What I believe is less known is the extent to which the valuation would be different in case Nebius just performs on that which it has announced and does not require a single contract or a single site approval.

I believe the potential upside to $220 is reachable, the downside is controllable, and risk-reward is skewed in the right direction to the point of declaring it a Buy.

data.title: Celestica: The Market Is Missing What Alphabet Just Confirmed
data.description:
Celestica Inc.’s (CLS) slight dip in pre-market trading Monday (April 20) following reports of a potential chip collaboration between Marvell (MRVL) and Alphabet/Google (GOOG/GOOGL) highlights the stock’s continued sensitivity to risks of revenue share dilution in the emerging custom silicon opportunity. This resembles a similar pullback in January leading up to Celestica’s Q4 earnings update, which was triggered by reports alleging Google’s diversification of its TPU server assembly orders to Taiwanese manufacturers like Inventec.

However, recent speculation of a potential Marvell-Google partnership on next-generation TPUs and, potentially, memory processing units (“MPUs”) could represent good news for Celestica. Recall from Celestica’s latest Q4 earnings update, the company had assuaged market concerns about potential revenue share loss by confirming an expanded partnership with Google as the hyperscaler’s preferred manufacturer of TPU systems. This expanded opportunity is reinforced by recent reports of a potential Google-Marvell partnership, which underscores the hyperscaler’s extension of its custom silicon portfolio beyond TPUs and into MPUs in order to address the intensifying memory crunch in ongoing AI infrastructure deployments. Google’s latest introduction of its next-generation TPU v8 processors, which are split into two variants – namely, 8i for inference and 8t for training – also underscore an expanded custom silicon roadmap poised to benefit the expanded Celestica system manufacturing partnership.

Taken together, Celestica remains well-positioned for continued appreciation, as it remains at the early stages of benefitting from increasing custom silicon and the adjacent networking opportunities discussed in the previous coverage.

The Significance of Celestica’s Partnership With Google

The Celestica stock has historically been sensitive to headlines that suggest Google diversification of its vendor list for TPU systems. This reflects the significance of Google as an anchor hyperscaler customer of Celestica’s fast-growing Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (“CCS”) business, which primarily consists of Hardware Platform Solutions (“HPS”), custom and networking sales to both communications (including hyperscaler) and enterprise end-markets. Specifically, Google’s partnership with Celestica represents a key qualitative sign of technological validation ahead of a secular shift towards custom silicon and adjacent networking opportunities – especially with the hyperscaler’s proprietary TPU systems currently representing one of the most successful in-house silicon against merchant alternatives like Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs. This likely explains why Celestica has already experienced three Google-related bouts of volatility this year, while its fundamental outlook remains intact:

The Pre-Earnings Drop in January

Hyperscalers have become a key customer cohort for Celestica as it expands its foray into AI infrastructure opportunities through its CCS segment offerings in recent years. Despite management’s reference to Celestica’s “decade-long partnership with Google” during the Q4 earnings call, the earliest disclosure of these collaborations was in the company’s 2022 10-K filing. Specifically, under the filing’s customer concentration disclosure, it specified hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), and Google as Celestica’s key CCS customers for the year ended December 31, 2022 – coinciding with the blockbuster debut of ChatGPT. Yet Google has been a particular standout over the past year.

Although Celestica hasn’t made any other direct references to its Google partnership until its recent Q4 earnings update earlier this year, the stock has been a key beneficiary of the hyperscaler’s TPU momentum acquired over the past year. Specifically, the Celestica stock’s close to five-fold upsurge over the past year has largely been in tandem with the doubling of Google’s market cap since the launch of TPU v7 “Ironwood” last April. This momentum was further reinforced by Anthropic’s expanded commitment to an additional 1 million units of Google’s TPU compute capacity starting this year, as well as the debut of TPU-trained Gemini 3 to “rave reviews” in November.

Yet the Celestica stock suffered from a pullback in late January amid reports that Google was diversifying its manufacturer roster for TPU servers into other Taiwanese companies like Inventec. This was a valid concern at the time, as any dilution of Celestica’s hyperscaler revenue share – especially amid rising interest in Google’s TPUs – could impact its core CCS segment’s growth and earnings outlook, risking a break in the stock’s momentum.

The Post-Earnings Uptick

The concerns were put to rest when management confirmed an expanded partnership with Google as the hyperscaler’s preferred manufacturer of TPU systems during the Q4 earnings update. Specifically, management made reference to the extension of Celestica’s “decade-long partnership with Google” by expanding its manufacturing capacity in the U.S. to “support the production of complex data center hardware, including Google TPU systems.” Management also confirmed Celestica’s status as Google’s preferred TPU manufacturer, as well as supplier of “advanced technical capabilities for the continued evolution of the TPU platform.”

The partnership is a key driver of visibility into the anticipated utilization of Celestica’s planned expansion of its global manufacturing capacity in 2026 and 2027. Not only so, but the technical component of the expanded partnership also complements momentum in Celestica’s fast-growing CCS segment. The expanded collaboration underscores potential adjacent networking opportunities as the company ramps up its 800G switches and prepares for the upcoming launch of next-generation 1.6T switches later this year. This collectively reinforces upside prospects to Celestica’s CCS growth and earnings profile, which likely underpinned the stock’s post-earnings recovery from the late January selloff.

The Google-Marvell Scare

Recent news of a potential Google-Marvell partnership on next-generation TPUs and MPUs has once again renewed concerns about revenue share dilution for Celestica, as it implies diversification of the hyperscaler’s custom silicon opportunity. However, based on the understanding that Celestica remains the preferred manufacturing partner of Google’s TPU servers, the potential Marvell partnership is likely a reinforcement of further upside.

Specifically, the alleged prospects of a Google-Marvell partnership point to not only the continuation of next-generation TPU developments but also the expansion of the hyperscaler’s custom silicon portfolio into MPUs. This expanded portfolio would align with Celestica’s ambitions to “support the growing demand for next-generation AI infrastructure” – particularly the “production of complex data center hardware” – and improve visibility into the utilization of its upcoming capacity expansion through 2027.

Also, it’s important to note that the potential Marvell partnership with Google will likely focus on scale-up opportunities, rather than scale-out networking that Celestica’s HPS program and switching roadmap target. Specifically, recent reports anticipate the alleged partnership to support two new chips – namely, a MPU “intended to work alongside Google’s TPU,” and an inference-specific TPU that’ll succeed the latest TPU 8i. This would align with Marvell’s deepening push into scale-up PCIe Gen 6 retimers and CXL memory-attach solutions, which have been bolstered by its recent acquisition of XConn.

Specifically, Marvell has been deepening its foray into scale-up opportunities – which refers to in-server and CPU-attached networking – with its PCIe Gen 6 retimers and CXL-related attach solutions.

The retimers are typically used to “recondition” degraded communication signals being transferred between servers by recovering and retransmitting cleaner data. This makes PCIe retimers critical for preserving cross-server interconnect reliability at increasingly high bandwidth and transmission speeds required by large-scale AI cluster deployments.

Meanwhile, Marvell’s custom compute express link (“CXL”) solutions are also becoming increasingly critical amid the intensifying memory crunch in AI infrastructure deployments. The company’s CXL-related attach solutions include near-memory accelerators and memory-expansion controllers that enable the use of recycled DRAM memory modules with new XPUs, GPUs, and CPUs. Coupled with the integration of XConn’s expertise in “advanced PCIe and CXL switching silicon,” Marvell is well positioned to capitalize on custom XPU-attach program wins with hyperscalers – such as Google’s potential foray in in-house MPUs in the future.

Meanwhile, Celestica’s networking roadmap primarily targets scale-out opportunities. As discussed in my previous coverage on the stock, Celestica’s ramp of its 800G top-of-rack (or “leaf”) switches and upcoming launch of the 1.6T switches align favorably with demands in scaling increasingly large AI cluster deployments. Specifically, the transition to the million-XPU cluster deployments is poised to create power and bandwidth bottlenecks, intensifying the requirement for high-speed networking fabrics to optimize infrastructure performance and token unit economics.

Implications of Google's TPU 8i and 8t Launch

This collective setup is expected to align favorably with Google’s TPU roadmap, as evidenced by the hyperscaler’s recent introduction of its next-generation 8i and 8t processors. Specifically, the training-focused TPU 8t is designed to scale “9,600 chips in a single superpod” with “maximum throughput across hundreds of superpods,” highlighting intensive scale-up and scale-out opportunities that’d benefit the existing Celestica partnership. The emerging strategic alignment with Celestica’s high-bandwidth switching portfolio is further corroborated by Google’s confirmation that the latest technology generation can “scale to more than 1 million TPU chips in a single training cluster.”

Google’s diversifying custom silicon portfolio also highlights increasing hyperscaler focus on non-GPU and non-merchant processors. This potentially reinforces prospects of incremental growth for Celestica’s CCS portfolio, especially as its HPS program gains momentum alongside adjacent networking offerings to support the rising demand for faster, higher-bandwidth, and lower-latency scale-out interconnects in large-scale AI cluster deployments.

Fundamental and Price Considerations

Based on recent industry developments, which reinforce prospects of an accelerating demand and capacity utilization backdrop for Celestica’s high-growth CCS segment, I’m updating my upside scenario price for the stock to $440 per share (previously $410 per share).

The stock’s outlook likely remains skewed to the upside, as the industry’s increasing diversification into custom silicon to support AI inference, alongside secular scale-out networking opportunities, underscores incremental growth to Celestica’s CCS portfolio. My updated upside scenario embeds a five-year revenue CAGR of 31% between 2025 and 2030 (previously 28%). This is primarily driven by upward-adjusted expectations in Celestica’s CCS upside scenario growth profile, which I now expect to expand at a five-year CAGR of 36% through 2030 compared to the previous projection of 33%.

These assumptions support an updated upside scenario price target of $440 per share. The upside scenario price target is computed under the discounted cash flow analysis with consideration of the above fundamental projections while maintaining the key valuation assumptions (i.e., 1.5% perpetual growth rate and 9.4% WACC) unchanged from the previous coverage.

Risks to Consider

The stock’s volatility experienced this year highlights inherent sensitivity to key customer dynamics – especially Google’s custom silicon trajectory and vendor-related developments, given the TPU’s particularly successful use case and adoption momentum compared to similar in-house developments by its hyperscaler peers like Amazon’s Trainium and Inferentia processors, Meta’s MTIA program, and Microsoft’s Maia accelerators (MSFT). Given the stock’s connection with Google’s upsurge over the past year, as well as the recent headlines pertaining to the hyperscaler’s custom silicon portfolio leading up to the Q1 earnings season next week, Celestica remains prone to volatility – especially as its expanding earnings multiple premium raises execution expectations ahead. Any signs of faster capex deployment than growth realization and/or delays to its capacity expansion roadmap could raise ROI compression concerns, which its current valuation leaves limited room for.

Final Thoughts

Taken together, it’s likely the market’s still underappreciating the breadth of Celestica’s upside as Google – as well as its broader hyperscaler customer cohort – deepens their respective custom silicon roadmaps. This is corroborated by prospects of Google’s expansion of its in-house silicon developments beyond TPUs and into MPUs based on recent speculation of a potential Marvell partnership, as well as a deepening foray into inference chips like the latest TPU 8i to target “operational intensities” of rising agentic AI requirements.

More importantly, Google’s currently expanded custom silicon roadmap with the disaggregation of TPU 8i and 8t, as well as the speculative partnership with Marvell on MPUs, also reinforces visibility into utilization of Celestica’s expanding manufacturing capacity through 2027. The emerging opportunity, alongside increasing industry adoption of infrastructure powered by custom silicon, is also expected to strengthen the strategic relevance of Celestica’s adjacent switching roadmap.

This accordingly underscores a competitive advantage for Celestica’s product and technology portfolio, which strategically aligns with hyperscalers’ increasing diversification into non-GPU architectures and increasingly large-scale AI cluster deployments. Despite risks of near-term volatility, Celestica’s fundamental outlook remains intact and well-positioned for further upside, as it remains in the early stages of monetizing a multi-year shift in AI architectures towards custom silicon adoption and higher bandwidth networking fabrics.

data.title: BridgeBio Pharma: Genetic Disease Specialist Is Thriving - I'm Upgrading To Buy
data.description:
Investment Overview

In February last year, I downgraded the stock of BridgeBio Pharma, Inc. (BBIO) from Buy to Hold in a note for Seeking Alpha. As it turned out, this was a bad call, because nearly 15 months on, shares have risen in value by very nearly 130%, trading at a value of $33 pre-market today. BridgeBio's market cap valuation is now $14.43bn.

Let me briefly explain some of the reasons why I downgraded to Hold.

I noted that BridgeBio had secured approval for its drug candidate acoramidis - now marketed and sold as Attruby in the indication of cardiomyopathy of wild-type or variant transthyretin-mediated amyloidosis ("ATTR-CM")—and noted that, despite likely intense competition against Pfizer's (PFE) Vyndamax (higher dose)/Vynadqel (tafamidis) and Alnylam's (ALNY) Ammvuttra (vutrisiran), the product was likely to one day drive "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) revenues, and perhaps as much as ~$4bn per annum.

I also wrote:

The broad label that will be attached to Attruby, which will state the oral drug reduces risk of both death and hospitalization, is a major boost for BridgeBio, as it is the same as Vyndaqel's, and BridgeBio has set the price of its drug slightly lower than Vyndaqel - $18,759, or ~$244k annually, compared to ~$268k for Pfizer's drug.

The two drugs have the same mechanism of action ("MoA"), with both being transthyretin (TTR) stabilizers, although both companies - unsurprisingly - claim their product is superior. The reality may be that there may not be much to choose between them, which will make it challenging for the newer drug to take market share away from the older, especially given the relative size and scale of each company's marketing and distribution infrastructures.

I noted that BridgeBio's market cap stood at >$6bn and suggested that:

For me, that is a big number for a company that will do very well to squeeze >$500m of revenues from its only commercial product in 2025, I'd estimate, with profitability unlikely. In fact, I would not be surprised if BridgeBio management opt to raise more funds in 2025 to fund its commercial launch, and to fund ongoing studies of its pipeline candidates.

I concluded that:

BridgeBio is unlikely to provide strong value for investors looking for gains in 2025, or possibly in 2026 either. It will take some time before we can see what impact Attruby will have on sales of Vyndaqel, and what impact the likely approval of Amvuttra in March will have on BridgeBio's share price.

Clearly, many investors felt otherwise and have piled into BridgeBio stock, so in this note, I will provide updates on the commercial progress of Attruby, pipeline progress, and funding and will try to see if there is justification for BridgeBio's current valuation - whether a downward correction may be likely or, conversely, whether there is further upside for the stock to explore.

Attruby's Strong Commercial Start Undermined By Competition, Losses, Lack Of Guidance

For Q4 2025, BridgeBio reported revenues of $154.2m, and for the full year 2025, revenues of $502.1m, almost exactly the figure I had predicted in my last note, although Attruby itself earned $146m in Q4 and $362.3m for the full year, the remaining revenue being derived from licensing and services.

Matt Outten, Chief Commercial Officer of BridgeBio, noted:

Attruby delivered 35% quarter-over-quarter growth in net product revenue in Q4, driven by its differentiated profile as the only near-complete stabilizer on the market, continued prescribing growth, repeat use, and patient persistence that has exceeded our expectations.

For context, in 2025, Vyndamax/Vyndaqel earned ~$6.4bn of revenues, up 17% year-on-year, and Amvuttra earned $2.3bn of revenues, up 138% year-on-year (the drug is also approved to treat hereditary amyloidosis). It's worth noting that Pfizer is planning to discontinue lower-dose Vyndaqel in 2025 in the U.S., as its patents are set to expire in 2028, and the Pharma hopes to encourage patients to switch to Vyndamax, whose patents do not expire until ~2035.

That seemingly places Attruby a distant third in the battle for market share in ATTR-CM, which could be slightly concerning. Unlike Vyndamax, an oral therapy taken once daily, Attruby is taken twice daily, while Amvuttra is administered via a once-every-three-month infusion.

The company has not - so far as I am aware - provided any revenue guidance for 2026, and longer-term, when cheaper generic versions of Vyndaqel come to market around 2028, would patients be prepared to switch to the cheaper drug over Attruby?

In 2025, BridgeBio reported R&D expenses of $452m, and SG&A expenses of $531m, and the company noted that:

For the three months and year ended December 31, 2025, the Company recorded a net loss attributable to common stockholders of BridgeBio of $(192.9m) and $(724.9m), respectively, compared to $(265.1m) and $(535.8m), respectively, for the same periods in the prior year.

In summary, then, we have a >$14bn market cap company earning ~$500m in revenues in 2025, making a net loss of ~$(725m), and providing no guidance for 2026, while its only commercial product making meaningful revenues could be threatened by cheaper generic competitors as soon as 2028.

BridgeBio Pipeline

If I'd had insight into full-year 2025 revenues and losses, plus no 2026 guidance when BridgeBio stock traded at ~$33 and was valued at a market cap of <$6.5bn, I would have been even more confident in my Hold recommendation and perhaps even recommended selling stock; however, BridgeBio is about much more than Attruby - the slide below, taken from a corporate presentation, illustrates the company's full product portfolio and pipeline:

While revenues from its other two commercial products are negligible (see my last note), the pipeline contains several highly promising assets. BridgeBio shares a helpful overview of progress and upcoming catalysts in its 2025 earnings press release, as shown below:

Let's start with BBP-418, indicated for limb-girdle muscular dystrophy, an inherited neuromuscular disorder that can result in loss of ambulation as well as respiratory decline and cardiac dysfunction. There are currently no approved therapies to treat the disease.

BBP-418 is a small molecule therapy, taken orally, that "targets LGMD2I/R9 at its source by restoring glycosylation of alpha-dystroglycan," BridgeBio says, and the company also reports in its 2025 10K filing that:

In October 2025, we reported positive topline results from the planned interim analysis of FORTIFY, a global, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled Phase 3 study evaluating BBP-418 in individuals with LGMD2I/R9. The FORTIFY interim analysis met all primary and key secondary endpoints, demonstrating statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvements across biomarker and functional measures, with a favorable safety profile.

Based on the FORTIFY interim analysis results and prior regulatory interactions, we are engaging with the FDA as we plan for submission of an NDA in the first half of 2026. If approved, BBP-418 may be eligible for a Priority Review Voucher under the Rare Pediatric Disease program. The FORTIFY study remains ongoing, with additional data collection and longer-term follow-up continuing.

A priority review voucher could shorten the FDA's review period from 10 months to six; therefore, we may speculate BBP-418 could be approved in early 2027, let's say, and with a patient population BridgeBio estimates to be ~7k, if we speculate a list price for the drug of ~$250k per annum, the market opportunity would be ~$1.75bn.

The good news is that, as we can see above, BBP-418 has demonstrated clinically meaningful improvements in patients in fields such as walk distance and pulmonary function, while many neuromuscular drug candidates have been approved based on surrogate endpoints, suggestive of clinical benefit. As such, I'd make the approval of this candidate more likely than not, and, if approved, speculate that "blockbuster" sales of the drug may be achievable.

As we can see, however, BridgeBio expects to submit two more New Drug Applications ("NDAs") in 2026. The first is for encaleret in the rare genetic disease Autosomal Dominant Hypocalcemia Type 1 ("ADH1"), for which there are no disease-modifying therapies approved - BridgeBio says conventional therapy currently consists of oral calcium and/or activated vitamin D supplementation.

As we can see above, the CALIBRATE pivotal study seems to have been a resounding success, and analysts have suggested this could be another billion-dollar per-annum revenue opportunity in waiting, especially given the expansion opportunity into chronic hypoparathyroidism, a ~200k patient market, BridgeBio believes.

Finally, there is infigratinib in the indication of achondroplasia (dwarfism) - results from the pivotal Phase 3 study PROPEL 3 were discussed by management as follows:

PROPEL 3 for oral infigratinib successfully met its primary endpoint (p<0.0001). The change from baseline in AHV (annualized height velocity) was superior to placebo at Week 52, with a mean treatment difference against placebo of +2.10 cm/year; topline results showed the first statistically significant improvements in body proportionality in achondroplasia

BioMarin's (BMRN) Voxzogo is approved to treat achondroplasia, earning ~$927m in revenues last year, so, after this pipeline review, we may conclude that BridgeBio could be marketing and selling four drugs with "blockbuster" potential by this time next year, two of which may have the potential to secure follow-on approvals in even larger patient markets.

Analysis: What's The Play On BridgeBio Today?

BridgeBio's stock gains across the past year of >115% have been characterized by steady growth as opposed to sudden spikes - although stock jumped from ~$55 to >$66 when the BBP-418 pivotal data were shared in October last year. Stock also slipped in value, from ~$78 per share to $63, after the company announced a private offering of ~$550m 0.75% convertible notes due 2033 in February.

It's hard not to be impressed by the progress BridgeBio has been making or its approach, illustrated below, based on leveraging scientific breakthroughs to treat genetic diseases.

Was I, therefore, wrong to downgrade BridgeBio stock in February last year? I have to admit that I think so today, although we should note the bull case does come with caveats.

As discussed, although management seems to be delighted with the launch of Attruby (also known as Beyonttra in the EU, where it has also been approved), Amvuttra and Vyndamax appear to be comfortably out-selling it today, while generic drug approvals are on the horizon.

Nevertheless, perhaps when BridgeBio shares its Q1 earnings in a few days' time at the end of this month, revenues and market share may have improved significantly - more positive data released at the end of March may help management establish its drug as the "best-in-class" against its rivals, although its dosing regime arguably counts against it.

BridgeBio also loses money - ~$500m per annum of net losses have been reported in each of the last six years - however the potential addition of three new commercial assets in 2027 may change that dynamic - in fact it will need to, because as of the end of 2025, BridgeBio reported cash and investments of $588m and long-term debt of $1.85bn.

What stands out to me across the past year is the quality of clinical data that BridgeBio has shared - three successful pivotal studies that seem to have delivered quality data across the board, with no problematic safety or efficacy flaws to have to work around in a regulatory submission. In my experience, it is quite rare to see so many "clean" study wins, and that may make it easy for the FDA to hand out three approvals across the next 12 months or so.

Re: Attruby, it also seems clear that management is determined to prove that its near-complete stabilization of TTR serum, early effect onset, and ability to reduce all-cause mortality by >40% and hospitalization by >50% will eventually make it physicians first choice - it is apparently the least expensive ATTR therapy today, costing a maximum of $175 per month for Medicare Part D patients, BridgeBio says, so perhaps I have been overemphasizing the generic threat.

All things considered, then, I'll have to admit I wish I had updated my rating on BridgeBio stock earlier, as the sets of pivotal study data rolled in in the second half of 2026, as today, while acknowledging some risks, I'd say this company can target ~$5bn in peak annual revenues by 2030, potentially.

In such a scenario, if profitability can be achieved also, there is no reason the company could not trade at 4/5x peak annual revenues, i.e., a market cap valuation of $20bn or $25bn, implying shares may still have ~50% upside potential.

With multiple catalysts upcoming in terms of approval submissions and more data from other pipeline assets, this may not be a bad time to think about accumulating some stock, although if, as seems likely, BridgeBio opts to raise more funding, it may be possible to buy in at a cheaper price around that time.

data.title: Meta Vs. Google: In The Age Of AI, The Ad Crown Is Up For Grabs
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) is poised to surpass Alphabet, Inc. (GOOGL)’s digital advertising revenue for the first time in history (see chart below). Today, ahead of Q1 2026 earnings, I am comparing the strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats the two companies face in regard to their (core) advertising business.

I will also discuss valuations and which company is, in my opinion, better positioned to emerge as a winner in the AI age. For the sake of readability, I will use both “Alphabet” and “Google” to refer to the company’s software ecosystem and advertising business.

Meta and Google: A History of Advertising Dependency

Meta and Alphabet are among the most innovative Tech companies in the world. Both have heavily invested in developing an ecosystem of Apps and software that locks in users for hours each day (I will cover the nuances and differences between the two ecosystems later in this article).

Yet, even after years of R&D, the two companies still show a heavy dependence on digital advertising. The share of revenue deriving from advertising has remained roughly stable for Meta for the past decade, at above 97%. For Google, the situation is a bit better: their Ad dependency decreased from 88% to 76% as of the last quarter, a result of the company’s push towards Cloud solutions and subscriptions.

Still, Google generates more than half of its revenue from Search Ads (56% as of Q4 2025), a product that was launched in the year 2000.

Both Companies Are “Victims” of a Self-reinforcing, Ad-centric Cycle

I think the powerful ecosystems of Meta and Alphabet are simultaneously a strength and a long-term threat for both companies. On the one hand, it is because of these ecosystems that both companies offer, today, the most efficient advertising solutions on Earth. Advertising is only as efficient as it can be precisely targeted. Google and Meta know everything about users' habits, behaviors, and interests, thanks exactly to their ecosystem.

On the other hand, the two companies have entered a sort of vicious cycle. Given their dependency on advertising, they have primarily focused on monetizing their ecosystems via more advertising. This, in turn, has created yet more dependency on advertising. In other words, it has made it very difficult for Google and Meta to find different revenue streams.

This is, in my opinion, particularly noticeable in the case of Google. The company is the third largest cloud provider on Earth. The “subscriptions, platforms, and devices” segment, together with the “Cloud” business, generated a staggering $31 billion in Q4 last year. None of these segments were material contributors 10 years ago.

Yet, because the company has been so good in simultaneously growing their advertising business, roughly three quarters of their revenue still depend on Ads.

Ad Dependency is Not a Bad Thing, but…

Advertising is an industry that is very sensitive to economic cycles. Advertisers tend to cut expenses in expectation of recessions. And marketing budgets also tend to lag behind other expenses when economic times improve.

Google and Meta have so far benefitted from a secular bull market trend that never really saw a deep and prolonged recession since 2008. But in case of economic disruption, potentially coming from AI putting people out of jobs, their core business would be very exposed.

Furthermore, advertising is also an industry that has historically seen fast changes. Google and Meta have been disruptors of this industry, and they have managed so far to stay relevant through changes in digital advertising. Yet, this is another potential risk of having such an ad-dependent business.

Overall, it is difficult to argue that a more diversified business would not be preferable for shareholders, especially in the AI age.

Google’s “Pull” Advertising Ecosystem Used to Be Its Moat

To understand Alphabet’s ecosystem, one has to start from the core product of the company: Search. Google Search has evolved significantly in the past decades, and the picture below outlines the latest evolution of “real estate” on a Google Search page. But at its core, one principle remains: sponsored results (ads) on Google Search appear based on the queries of users and are highly relevant to them.

This, in marketing, is called a “pull” type of advertising. Ads are served when users are coming (pulling) to you, which is a moment of high interest. This is opposed to traditional “push” advertising, which means serving Ads more or less regardless of what they are doing. A classic example is advertising billboards.

The entire ecosystem of Google, in my opinion, has always followed the spirit of Search. Google tends to offer (mostly) free software that allows the company to track users' behaviors and serve them relevant Ads at the highest moment of interest. Google’s Android, Gmail, Drive (Cloud), and Chrome, for example, all allow Google to have a deep knowledge of what users want and when they want it.

With the notable exception of YouTube, users of Google’s ecosystem do not necessarily consume content (as opposed to those of Meta’s ecosystem). They use Google’s solutions for their work and personal lives, exposing a somewhat more “intimate” side to Google itself.

But Google Ads Are Losing Their Edge in the AI Age

Pull advertising used to be Google’s moat. There really were (and still are) not many other ecosystems where users can be served Ads at the exact moment they are expressing an interest in a topic. An exception is Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN)’s advertising, which serves Ads at a moment when users are looking for products on their eCommerce.

But the moat of Google’s “pull” type advertising has, in my opinion, been eroded by AI. Barriers between “pull” and “push” advertising have been blurred in recent years, thanks to ever-more reliable and precise AI predictions of users’ behaviors. Meta has been at the forefront of this transformation, and has accelerated its advertising business significantly as a result.

Meta’s “Push” Advertising Ecosystem Has Been Boosted by AI

Meta’s advertising solutions have always been of a “push” type. Users are served (pushed) ads while engaging with content within Meta’s ecosystem. This is a more intuitive type of advertising, not dissimilar in principle to Television advertising.

What changes against TV ads (and where Meta excels) is the high effectiveness of Meta’s solutions. Traditionally, Meta’s “push” type of advertising would have meant less effectiveness, as ads rely on users' inferred interests (from what they do in Meta’s ecosystem) rather than behavior predicting what they exactly want. This gap is now closing with AI, in my view.

Meta’s Ads are increasingly driven by its Advantage+ suite, which shifts the burden of audience targeting from the advertiser to Meta's algorithms. Instead of marketers manually guessing which demographic might buy their product, machine learning models process billions of daily data points (from Reel watch times to subtle engagement cues) to autonomously find the highest-converting users in real-time.

In practice, this means Meta's algorithms can now anticipate a user's purchasing intent before that user ever types a related query into Google. By turning passive discovery into actionable intent, Meta is effectively eroding the historical advantage of Google's "pull" moat.

Meta has also integrated generative AI tools directly into its Ad Manager to handle image expansion and text variations. This lowers the friction and cost of A/B testing, particularly for small and medium-sized businesses that make up the bulk of Meta's client base.

Meta’s Relentless Focus on Advertising is Paying Dividends

Comparing Ad metrics between Google and Meta confirms the growth story for the latter company. As shown in the chart below, Meta’s growth in Price Per Ad has grown at a higher pace than Google’s CPC (Cost-per-Click, its equivalent) for almost two years now. The number of impressions (i.e., ads “served” to users) has also grown ahead of Google’s for the better part of the last five years.

I believe the reason is exactly the fact that Meta figured out how to use AI to improve the effectiveness of their advertising solutions, closing the “gap” with Google’s “pull” ecosystem.

When advertisers see a structurally higher ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) because AI is handling both the creative optimization and the targeting, they tend to allocate a larger share of their marketing budgets to Meta, which is what, in my view, ultimately drove Meta overtaking Alphabet’s digital advertising by revenue.

A Quick Note About “Interests” and “Behaviors”

Note that the way I am using the words “interest” and “behavior” in this article does not necessarily correspond to the way this nomenclature is used in the marketing industry. Meta does offer what they call “behavior based” targeting, and so does Google. For the sake of simplicity, I am using “interests” to refer to a “push” type of advertising. I am using “behaviors” to refer to a “pull” type of advertising.

Google’s Ecosystem: A SWOT Analysis

After explaining the differences between the two ecosystems, I find it useful to build a SWOT analysis of both companies’ ecosystems to recap what I just outlined in one framework.

Strengths

Weaknesses

Opportunities

Threats

Ad solutions still rank as the most effective.

High dependence on Search as the centerpiece of the ecosystem.

Solid base for diversification from Advertising.

Meta's AI-driven "push" ads are eroding Google's historical "pull" moat.

"Pull" ecosystem captures users at their highest moment of interest.

Ad-centric vicious cycle stifles focus on alternative revenue streams.

High-margin growth potential in Cloud, subscriptions, and platforms.

Fairly sensitive to economic cycles and recessionary ad budget cuts.

Deep ecosystem integration (Android, Gmail, Chrome) offers intimate behavioral data.

Overall ad dependency remains high (76%) despite enterprise segments.

High-intent eCommerce queries are increasingly captured by Amazon.

Meta’s Ecosystem: A SWOT Analysis

Strengths

Weaknesses

Opportunities

Threats

All-in approach to advertising paid off: Meta is overtaking Google.

Extreme revenue concentration, with >97% tied directly to advertising.

Structurally higher ROAS naturally captures a larger share of marketer budgets.

Very sensitive to economic cycles and recessionary ad budget cuts..

Advantage+ AI accurately anticipates purchasing intent before a search occurs.

Lack of enterprise or cloud computing segments to fall back on.

Fully closing the performance gap between "push" and "pull" advertising.

Macroeconomic disruptions (e.g., AI-driven job displacement) impacting consumer spending.

Unrivaled content ecosystem that locks in user attention for hours daily.

Bringing It All Together: Which Company Will Win in the AI Race?

I think Meta is successfully leveraging AI to erode the moat of Google’s “pull” advertising ecosystem. Today, Meta’s Ads are not yet as effective as those of Google, but they are getting close. This has been the result of a relentless focus on improving advertising, which constitutes 97%+ of Meta’s revenue.

Google, on the other hand, may have lost a bit of its focus on advertising because the company is focused on growing new revenue streams, such as Cloud and Services. Growth has been great for those new segments. But the sheer size of Google’s advertising acted like a counterweight to that growth and made the company fall victim to its own success in advertising.

Ultimately, I believe both Google and Meta can be winners in the AI race, in different ways. Google can be an AI winner if it leverages AI to finally diversify its business away from advertising, unlocking new revenue streams and high growth.

Meta can be a winner if it accelerates further its advertising business, positioning itself as the undisputed leader of advertising in the AI age. Meta’s AI and Metaverse bets can, of course, represent another potential upside.

Meta is Cheaper Than Google and My Slight Favorite Between the Two

I hope my analysis of Meta and Alphabet’s ecosystems sheds some light on which business I think is the most resilient and better positioned today. These considerations, however, would serve little purpose if not discussed together with the companies’ current market valuations. The table below compares key financial figures and valuation metrics of the two companies.

By most metrics, Meta is the most profitable and cheapest business to buy. Google is far more expensive, trading at a FWD P/E ratio of ~28X. To be clear, I think the market is assigning this premium to Google because of its leadership in AI: Google’s Gemini AI model is among the best-performing LLMs and miles ahead of Meta’s troubled AI efforts.

Why I Think Meta Has a Better Risk-Reward Profile

In a recent article, I outlined how I think there really are three types of AI companies: AI Enablers (infrastructure), AI Creators (model builders), and AI Adopters (application layer). I believe the next wave of alpha is likely to come from AI Adopters, i.e. those companies that follow a more AI-agnostic approach and are less exposed to CapEx.

Both Google and Meta are AI Creators and have committed significant CapEx to their respective AI efforts. This said, I think Meta has so far been able to better leverage AI overall than Google, despite lagging behind Google in AI models. This, in my view, positions it somewhat closer to the field of AI Adopters than AI Creators.

Given this context and the respective market valuations of the two companies, I find the risk/reward profile of Meta to be preferable than that of Google at the time of writing.

My Meta Long-Term Price Target: Between $1000 - $3,500 Per Share (Buy)

I last covered Meta in November with an exhaustive DCF-based valuation model for the company. For the sake of limiting the length of this article, I am not going to repeat my full thesis. I am however reporting the key financial metrics tied to my valuation model, along with my three valuation scenarios (bear, bull and base).

My base case, which sees Meta growing at current rates for the next 5 years, sees an upside up to $1,081 per share, stretching to $3,530 should the company manage to accelerate its growth from 25% to 35% by leveraging AI.

Why Google is a Hold for Me and What Upside I See in the Stock

While I prefer Meta overall in terms of risk profile, Google is by no means a company I would recommend to sell. Net of the evolution of its ecosystem that I just described today, there are at least three good reasons:

  1. Leadership in AI, with Gemini ranking among the top three LLMs at the time of writing.

  2. Proven track record of scaling new business segments (Cloud, Subscriptions).

  3. An advertising business that, while it may not have the same moat as before AI, is still growing double-digits and has not been disrupted by AI at all.

I ultimately recommend a HOLD for now because it remains unclear to what extent owning the best AI model can actually translate into a competitive advantage. This, combined with the somewhat rich valuation of Google, means, in my opinion, that most good news for the company is priced in by the market. In that sense, I do not see any upside in GOOGL until the AI situation becomes clearer.

Q1 Earnings Catalysts & Risks for GOOGL

The market narrative of Google is, in my opinion, primarily tied to the ability of the company to diversify its business from advertising. This is the only way Alphabet can prove they can capitalize on their AI leadership, and continue the market momentum to ignite a new bull run from current prices.

This is why the primary element I will look at for Q1 earnings is the pace of growth of Search Ads (and the advertising business) against the growth of cloud and subscriptions.

As the chart above illustrates, advertising has been growing at a slightly slower pace than the other two segments since Q1 2023 (roughly the time Google started using the current naming for their businesses in reporting).

This is generally a good sign, because it means that eventually the advertising business should be overtaken by the other segments. However, I want to see an acceleration in this growth differential because, at current paces, it would take 6+ years for advertising to be overtaken by cloud and subscriptions in size. A reversal of this trend would be the main risk at Q1 earnings, in my opinion.

Q1 Earnings Catalysts & Risks for META

The market narrative for Meta is mainly about advertising, at least for now. While I would like to see Meta announcing they can diversify their business, I don’t think this is a condition needed for the stock to perform well in the immediate future.

In this context, I will primarily look at the growth pace of advertising (and overall revenue, since ads account for 97%+ of revenue today) to gauge whether Meta is keeping up its outstanding results in leveraging AI for improving advertising targeting.

Given the heavy spend of Meta in AI CapEx (see chart below), any news about new revenue streams would represent a significant catalyst for the stock.

And here, once again, lies the difference between Google and Meta in the current market situation. While Google “needs” to show results within their AI strategy to continue its bull run, I think the market is more lenient with Meta. The stock trades at a far cheaper valuation than Google, and in this context, delivering results on their advertising business could be sufficient for a continued bull run.

Risks to My Thesis

Through this article, I have already outlined many elements of risk for GOOGL and META. The common risk denominator concerns a recession. Since both companies are heavily reliant on advertising, they are very exposed to advertising and marketing budget cuts, which historically tend to manifest ahead of recessions.

For Google, the key risk in not buying this stock is that it is equivalent to betting against the company’s AI strategy. As an AI bull myself, I recognize there could be potentially significant upside if the company manages to make AI a driver for business diversification.

For Meta, the key risk in buying this stock concerns its AI CapEx. The market will eventually want to see results for such investments, and any negative news in this regard will likely trigger a sell-off in the stock. Meta also has a troubled history with CapEx: its investments in the Metaverse have failed to return value, and the company has recently scaled them down significantly.

data.title: Mobia Medical Moves Forward On Its IPO Plan
data.description:
Mobia Medical Is Growing, But Operating Losses Are Heavy

Mobia Medical, Inc. (MOBI) has filed for an IPO to continue its commercialization efforts, according to an SEC S-1 registration statement.

The firm is commercializing a neurostimulation therapy device for stroke patients.

MOBI is growing quickly, but so are operating losses. In a potentially “higher for longer” interest rate environment that punishes high operating loss-making companies, valuation may be a concern.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn more about the IPO from management.

What Does Mobia Medical Do?

Mobia has developed an FDA-approved implantable vagus nerve stimulation device designed to assist chronic ischemic stroke patients in recovering and restoring motor function via neuroplasticity.

The company's primary system is the Vivistim Paired Vagus Nerve Stimulation System, which management says is the first and only clinically validated FDA-approved device for treating chronic ischemic stroke patients who have moderate to severe upper extremity impairment.

The device is implanted and delivers electric stimulation to the vagus nerve during functional movement to retrain muscles and restore function.

The firm says that since its full commercial launch in 2023, the device has been implanted in more than 1,000 patients, with 700 implants occurring in 2025.

Mobia cites results from its trial as follows:

In our pivotal, triple-blind, randomized, sham-controlled VNS-REHAB trial, Vivistim Therapy resulted in an approximately two-times greater, as measured by Fugl-Meyer Assessment of Upper Extremity (FMA-UE), and approximately three-times greater, as measured by Wolf Motor Function Test (WMFT), improvement in hand and arm function compared to intensive rehabilitation alone, at the end of the in-clinic and at-home therapy protocol.

(Source: SEC)

Management is led by President and Chief Executive Officer Richard Foust, who has been with the company since 2022.

Mr. Foust was previously Chief Commercial Officer at Velano Vascular, a medical device company, and Chief Business Officer at Analyte Health, a digital health company.

Investors in Mobia include U.S. Venture Partners, GPG Healthcare Opportunities, Osage University Partners, Synapse Investment, Cooperatieve Gilde Healthcare, Longitude Venture Partners, and others.

As of December 31, 2025, the firm had booked fair market value investments of approximately $187 million.

Subsequent to the 2025 cutoff date of the filed financials, Mobia issued an additional $40 million in convertible promissory notes.

What Is Mobia's Market?

Mobia operates in a subset of the larger neurostimulation device market, which was an estimated $5.8 billion in 2024 and was projected to exceed $13.9 billion by 2033, according to a market research report by Grand View Research.

If this growth is achieved, it would represent a CAGR of 10.3% from 2025 to 2033, a moderately robust rate of growth.

The market is expected to grow due to an increasing prevalence of neurological conditions, a desire for minimally invasive solutions, and a growing demand for pain management treatments for chronic conditions.

North America accounted for nearly 44% of the total market share of revenue in 2024.

The forecast global neurostimulation devices market growth trajectory through 2033 is shown in the chart below, divided by product type:

Competitors in the broader neurostimulation device market include:

  • Abbott (St. Jude Medical) (ABT).

  • Medtronic (MDT).

  • Boston Scientific (BSX).

  • Aleva Neurotherapeutics.

  • Nexstim.

  • LivaNova PLC (LIVN).

  • NeuroPace (NPCE).

  • Nevro Corporation.

  • Nalu Medical (NALU).

  • Mainstay Medical.

  • Saluda Medical Pty Ltd.

  • Biotronik.

  • electroCore (ECOR).

  • MicroTransponder.

  • TVNS Technologies GmbH.

  • Laborie.

  • Axonics.

Recent industry trends are marked by increasing government funding for neurological treatments to support research and development.

In the U.S., the expansion of value-based care and home-based therapy is also an important trend, although the industry has been hampered by reimbursement lags.

Mobia’s strengths are that it has a first-mover advantage, meaningful clinical validation, high patient satisfaction, and a scalable commercial model through stroke center placement.

Its weaknesses include limited reimbursement coverage, as most commercial insurers have classified its product as experimental or investigational and not covered; a single product (Vivistim); a surgical implantation requirement; and a high opex structure.

The company's opportunities lie with expanding reimbursement coverage, international market potential, and treating other indications beyond stroke.

Threats to Mobia’s prospects include competing neuromodulation devices, ponderous hospital adoption delays, regulatory risks, and reimbursement risks.

Mobia’s Recent Financial Results

Topline revenue and gross profit have more than doubled year-over-year, but so have operating losses and cash burn (nearly), as the company's recent financial results show here:
