Mobia’s IPO Plan

MOBI plans to sell shares of its common stock in an IPO that could exceed $100 million in size, excluding customary underwriter allotments.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180 days after the effective date of the prospectus and do not include any “leak out” provisions.

The firm plans to use the net proceeds of the IPO as follows:

to expand our direct sales force and commercial organization;

to continue our research and development activities and our clinical studies and trials; and

the remainder for working capital and other general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

Management’s online IPO roadshow presentation is not yet available.

Mobia said it is not currently the subject of any legal proceedings that it believes would have a material adverse effect on its business.

Underwriters of the IPO are listed as BofA Securities, J.P. Morgan, Goldman Sachs, BTIG, and Wolfe | Nomura Alliance.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of December 31, 2025, Mobia had approximately $33.6 million in cash and equivalents and $19.1 million in total liabilities, of which $7.1 million was debt.

As previously mentioned, an additional $40 million in convertible promissory notes were issued by the end of February 2026, increasing debt to around $47.1 million.

These convertible notes may also convert at a minimum 20% discount to the IPO price, creating direct dilution to existing shareholders and indirect dilution to new shareholders.

The signal from this financing is likely that the company needed the cash due to its high burn rate and may not have been able to get it from its existing investor base without a down-round valuation haircut.

Free cash flow for 2025 was negative ($46 million) on heavy opex losses but light capex.

The company is heavily investing in its operations, but without the IPO’s proceeds, it would likely have less than a year’s cash runway, so the IPO is likely a must-have outcome.

Gross margins are high at around 81%, so Mobia has strong unit economics.

In sum, management’s capital allocation is aimed at maximizing revenue growth, and the company's future is dependent on scaling revenue faster than SG&A expense growth.

Mobia Medical Is Growing Quickly, But Operating Losses Are High

Mobia is seeking public investment to fund its heavy operating expense structure as it ramps up sales and marketing for its Vivistim system.

The firm’s growth strategy entails targeting 1,500 stroke centers in the United States via a growing direct sales force while continuing its physician and patient market education efforts.

Management is also engaging with CMS and private insurers to expand reimbursement coverage while continuing to improve device usability and patient outcomes.

The market for neurostimulation devices is large and expected to grow at a moderately robust rate of growth, likely due to an aging global population suffering an increasing incidence of stroke and motor nerve impairment.

MOBI has a tight focus on stroke therapy, but its high gross margins are no doubt enticing to existing neurostimulation device makers who have deep pockets and large sales forces to ramp up quickly.

So, Mobia has the very real prospect of increasingly intense competition from other market participants.

The firm has significant product, supplier, customer, and reimbursement concentrations, leading to small points of failure or limitations on its growth potential.

Additional risks include management’s designation of the company as both a “smaller reporting company” and an “emerging growth company,” which enables Mobia to produce substantially less information for shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have disappointed IPO investors in post-IPO trading.

My overall summary of the Mobia IPO so far is:

  • Positives - Market focus, product differentiation, high gross margins, first-mover advantage.

  • Uncertain - Unproven scalability, not deeply entrenched.

  • Negatives - High cash burn, adoption curve changes, reimbursement challenges.

Management’s valuation assumptions at IPO will be critical to investor demand. The company's high operating losses may not bode well for that demand, especially in a “higher for longer” interest rate environment that penalizes operating loss-making companies like Mobia.

I’ll provide an update when we learn more IPO details.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined.

data.title: Japan Flash PMI Signals Record Price Rise And Fading Growth Amid Ongoing War In The Middle East
data.description:
The S&P Global Flash PMI® for Japan indicated the largest rise in selling prices recorded since comparable data were first available in 2007. Price hikes were often linked to the war in the Middle East, notably via energy prices and supply shortages. Supplier lead times lengthened in April to a degree nearly matching the extremes seen during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Some encouragement came from news of the fastest rise in Japanese manufacturing output for over 12 years in April. However, this good news was tainted by a cooling in service sector activity growth, as well as indications that the manufacturing upturn was in part a reflection of safety stock building as producers and their customers grew increasingly concerned over the price rises and supply shortages emanating from the war, hinting at weaker growth in the coming months. Business expectations worsened accordingly, pointing to the gloomiest outlook since 2020.

Growth fades further

The headline S&P Global Japan PMI Composite Output Index slipped from 53.0 at the end of the first quarter to 52.4 in April, according to the ‘flash’ reading. Although the survey indicates that business activity across Japan has now expanded in each of the past 13 months, accelerating to one of the fastest rates yet recorded by the survey in February, the expansion has slowed over the past two months since the outbreak of war in the Middle East.

While the growth rate remains encouragingly resilient at first glance, the sector details present a more concerning picture.

Resilience buoyed by stock building

Business activity across the service sector rose at only a modest pace that was the slowest in 11 months. That left the manufacturing sector as the main driver of the expansion. However, although factory output growth accelerated sharply to the highest since February 2014, anecdotal evidence provided by survey respondents indicated that manufacturers often raised output due to concerns over future supply shortages or raw material input price hikes due to the war in the Middle East.

Signs of the war impacting supply chains and purchasing behaviour were provided by several of the PMI’s sub-indices.

Suppliers’ delivery times lengthened in April to the greatest extent since May 2022, almost matching the worst readings seen at the height of the pandemic as shipping delays and shortages became more widely reported.

Meanwhile, in a rare development, inventories of raw material purchases were allowed to rise in April, up for the first time since last June and rising at a rate not exceeded for close to two years.

Record price surge

This additional purchasing, alongside spiking energy prices, drove input costs higher across both manufacturing and services. Average input costs faced by Japanese firms increased at the sharpest rate since January 2023 in April, while average output charges (or selling prices) rose at the quickest rate since data covering both goods and services were first available in late 2007. This points to a marked rise in consumer price inflation in the coming months.

Business confidence at lowest since pandemic

In this environment, business confidence regarding future output deteriorated for a second successive month in April, with overall optimism the lowest recorded since August 2020 during the COVID-19 pandemic (albeit well off the lows seen during the pandemic). Uncertainty and disruption to markets due to the war in the Middle East were the principal causes of dampened forecasts.

Original Post

data.title: Allspring Emerging Growth Fund Q1 2026 Performance Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Allspring Emerging Growth Fund Q1 2026 Commentary.

Fund performance and attribution

Top Contributors And Detractors To Quarter-End Fund Performance

Contributors

  • Modine Manufacturing Co. (MOD)
  • Carpenter Technology Corp. (CRS)
  • Argan, Inc. (AGX)
  • DigitalOcean Holdings, Inc. (DOCN)
  • AAR Corp. (AIR)

Detractors

  • iRhythm Holdings, Inc. (IRTC)
  • Genius Sports Ltd. (GENI)
  • ADMA Biologics, Inc. (ADMA)
  • Commvault Systems, Inc. (CVLT)
  • RadNet, Inc. (RDNT)

The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Discussion Of Contributors

Modine Manufacturing (MOD) moves full speed into AI-driven cooling. Modine designs mission-critical heating, cooling, and ventilation systems, with a growing focus on its climate solutions segment serving data centers. Shares rallied during the quarter as Modine beat financial expectations, driven by surging data center demand, improved profitability, and higher guidance. Management also announced a strategic shift away from legacy auto components, repositioning the company as a pure-play climate solutions and data center provider. This captured the attention of investors who had otherwise avoided the company. With accelerating demand for data center services, we feel that Modine is well positioned for sustained growth and margin expansion.

Tailwinds carry Carpenter Technology Corp. (CRS) to new heights. Carpenter Technology manufactures high-performance alloy metals used in aerospace, defense, and power generation. After years of capacity constraints across the aerospace industry, Carpenter Technology is benefiting from increased production rates at Boeing (BA) and recent regulatory approvals that are driving demand for mission-critical materials and components. Producing advanced alloys is both complex and capital intensive, giving Carpenter robust pricing power, which drove outsized earnings growth during the quarter. Carpenter remains well positioned to deliver strong performance through pricing, mix, and sustained aerospace demand.

Argan, Inc. (AGX), relieves skilled labor bottlenecks. Power has become a critical input to the massive AI infrastructure build-out and the labor required to increase power output is in short supply. In addition to AI, increases in electric vehicle adoption, manufacturing, and automation have sparked high demand for energy consumption—a condition the U.S. labor force is not currently equipped to accommodate. As one of only a few contractors capable of building large-scale power plants, Argan benefits from pricing power and strong demand across both gas-fired and renewable projects tied to grid modernization. During the quarter, Argan shares rallied, driven by stronger-than-expected margins and higher capacity additions, which helped ease concerns around tight labor and supply constraints. With clear visibility from backlogs, Argan is positioned for attractive growth, driven by the critical need for increased power output.

Discussion Of Detractors

iRhythm Holdings, Inc. (IRTC), skipped a beat in the quarter. The company offers a next-generation medical device for patients with heart irregularities. The company's monitor, called Zio, digitally monitors a patient's heart rate for up to two weeks and continuously reports data back to a doctor. As a superior alternative to the current standard level of care, iRhythm's devices are more likely to identify heart issues before they cause serious problems for patients. Despite beating the market's growth expectations and providing positive revenue guidance for 2026, iRhythm's stock was caught up in "AI doomerism" during the quarter as investors raised questions on the strength of the company's moat. For the time being, we view the valuation pressure as largely sentiment driven and unrelated to underlying fundamentals. We continue to hold the position and will closely monitor the competitive landscape.

Investors called their bets off for Genius Sports Ltd. (GENI). Genius Sports is a leading business-to-business sports data and technology provider that supplies official real-time data, low-latency video, and betting technology. The company excels at integrity monitoring to major sports leagues, sportsbooks, and media companies. The company is positioned to benefit from continued U.S. sports betting expansion, margin improvement, and the evolution of its media technology offerings. Despite solid top-line growth, shares performed poorly during the quarter as profitability concerns and broader risk-off sentiment weighed on the stock. This was compounded by skepticism surrounding the company's $1.2 billion acquisition of Legend and thus widely viewed as high risk and poorly timed. In response to heightened near-term uncertainty and execution risk related to mergers and acquisitions, we trimmed the position and are closely monitoring the fundamental outlook.

ADMA Biologics, Inc., faced a clot in cash conversion. ADMA is a biopharmaceutical company focused on plasma-derived immunoglobulin therapies for patients with immune deficiencies and select infectious disease risks, including RSV. Its portfolio includes company-owned plasma collection centers that secure supply and enable end-to-end manufacturing control. During the quarter, elevated inventories and slower cash conversion temporarily pressured working capital and "days sales outstanding" despite continued healthy end-market demand. These dynamics were exacerbated by a short seller report that heightened concerns, although management emphasized the transitory nature of the issues and expects normalization ahead. We maintained our position, supported by expectations for continued treatment adoption and strong margin expansion.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Russell 2000® Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 2000 companies with higher price/book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with lower price/book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with higher price/book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000® Index, which represents approximately 8% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000 Index. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition Of Terms:

Active share of an account is calculated by subtracting a portfolio's overlap with its performance benchmark from 100%. The portfolio-level overlap compares parent-level weights between positions held in both the portfolio and benchmark, summing the lesser of such weights.

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

Long-term growth forecast is the annual earnings-per-share growth that the company can sustain over the next 3 or 5 years based on Allspring estimate data.

Price/earnings to growth ratio (PEG ratio) uses long-term earnings growth estimates. The long-term earnings growth is the annual earnings-per-share growth that the company can sustain over the next 3 or 5 years based on estimate data.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Smaller company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than those of larger companies. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled small growth funds, the Emerging Growth Fund received 2 stars among 522 funds, 2 stars among 500 funds, and 3 stars among 399 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. The Morningstar Rating is for the Institutional Class only; other classes may have different performance characteristics.

data.title: Victory Capital Holdings: Another ~7% EBIT Lift From Synergies Incoming
data.description:
Victory Capital (VCTR) should see some reasonable AUM pickup since the end of March thanks to a reappreciation of the market for the moment, which are shrugging off the fact that the Iran War is still not resolved, and the Strait still not really open. Other than that, the case with VCTR are the synergies incoming from the Amundi US acquisition, now called Pioneer Investments. That alone could be almost a 7% EBIT lift with outstanding synergies to be gained. In terms of valuation, it's at 18x PE trailing. Considering a full year of Amundi AUM now under them and the synergies, and assuming a normal AUM bump in line with typical market growth, we get something like a forward 12x PE, consistent with the FWD PE given on Seeking Alpha. That's about in line with what we'd consider to be long-term reasonable AM multiples, and in line with some of the passive asset managers. But by no means a great bargain. Also in terms of direction, we are not sure the market is being reasonably by ignoring a progressing energy crunch, and an uncertain solution in peace talks that haven't taken place. Panic in the markets again, a threat if the Iran War hostilities continue, is not good for VCTR.

Results

Briefly, Victory is a pretty straightforward asset management company, operating through a couple of franchises, offering all sorts of products, but with a relatively market-in-line exposure overall. That is to say no outsized AUM contribution from exotic strategies, and likely a lot of quasi-indexed strategies contributing to the underlying.

The big thing with Victory has been its AUM developments as a consequence of a pretty transformative fold in of Amundi US, which was done without cash consideration but with an equity share. The Amundi acquisition was responsible for around a 33% growth in AUM alone, and since the close occurred mid-year, has only half contributed to revenue growth for the FY.

In exchange, Amundi is receiving around 26% of VCTR's overall share capital. This has already occurred as of July. In our view that is quite a good deal, considering the AUM contribution now and considering also the synergies that will be obtainable by VCTR as a consequence of the merger.

The integration work began before close, around $50 million of $105 million achieved, with another $30 million presumably achieved within the year, and therefore another $30 million to be achieved in the coming year. That's almost a 7% lift on the FY 2025 EBIT from outstanding synergies. In addition to that, with a full consolidated year of Amundi, now called Pioneer, we expect another 15% bump to sales growth from a full year of management fee contributions, assuming the Amundi fee profile is in line with the previous comprehensive VCTR fee profile. With market growth of the average 7% per year also factored in, we are looking at around a 35% EBIT lift, assuming no operating leverage.

Bottom Line

Related to the Iran War, the hits to the market were very visible in the latest 8-K on AUM disclosure - at least from February levels. The March levels were still more than 10% above the ending AUM, which is an accomplishment given YTD market performance.

We expect further recovery in AUM on appreciation from the March levels in the currently open quarter.

For the incoming results, we are still expecting to see solid AUM growth YoY, it should be around 10% as well, and that should drive growth and hopefully even more in operating income as the further $30 million in annualised synergies materialise.

As a consequence of the aforementioned 35% EBIT lift, assuming the same on net income, we can adjust down the trailing multiple of around 18% to around 12x. That's in line with State Street (STT) which should have an adjusted PE of around 13x. 10x is what we'd consider to be a typical AM (Asset Management) PE, something fair across developed geographies. The slight premium isn't too unreasonable, especially given the decent AUM growth, but we still feel that there is pressure from even lower cost industry alternatives and there are definitely cheaper options out there. Italian AM peers for example are below 10x in multiple, and they are not doing anything particularly differently. They all, however, are exposed in the event that the hostilities under the Iran War continue.

Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index for the first quarter that ended March 31, 2026.
  • Holdings within health care and consumer discretionary detracted from returns.
  • Security selection within industrials contributed to performance.

Market review

A tale of two markets

The first quarter of 2026 unfolded as a tale of two distinct environments, effectively split into prewar and postwar periods. Early in the quarter, markets were anticipating policy stimulus and monetary easing, creating a constructive backdrop for value investors as cyclical stocks performed impressively. This was fueled by wide valuation spreads and optimism for economic growth, with many economists expecting U.S. gross domestic product growth to exceed 3% in 2026. Historically, periods of accelerating economic growth shifts investor preference toward more economically sensitive sectors and stocks with lower valuations. This was the case in the prewar period.

The outbreak of war in Iran quickly unwound the prewar optimism. Many cyclical sectors sold off as oil prices spiked and risk appetites deteriorated. Investor positioning pivoted, favoring energy as the clear performer. The inflation picture became clouded as surging energy prices pressured consumer confidence and reduced the outlook for rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. Still, not all defensive sectors behaved as expected and the environment challenged traditional playbooks. Industrials held up despite elevated valuations, and this was due in part to their exposure to the build-out of artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure. Technology and other high-growth sectors weakened amid general de-risking, while health care's lackluster performance stood out as a surprise in the postwar “risk off” sentiment. Throughout March, the on-again, off-again events surrounding the Iran war loomed large in the psyche of investors.

AI “doomerism” and the “SaaSocalypse”

The quarter was also marked by the so-called AI “doomerism” trade. This flared as investors reassessed whether fast-improving and increasingly autonomous agentic AI can erode the moats of software, data, and white-collar services. The spark started with Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) Claude/Cowork rollout and was amplified by OpenAI’s updated Codex, which enables users to “vibe code” applications from scratch with relatively simple prompts. In late February, a colorful Substack blog from Citrini Research received notable attention as it fanned the flames of the sell-off with a vivid premonition of agentic AI’s eventual disruption of the global economy. Investors responded by selling first and asking questions later. The first victims were legacy providers in legal research and publishing, then a widening set of application software, financial data, and exchange operators, and even IT outsourcing, erasing roughly $300 billion in value. At the end of the quarter, software and IT services were among the year’s weakest industries.

The anxiety was magnified by how much capital—both public and private—had been invested around previously believed “sticky” software-as-a-service (SaaS) contracts with recurring revenue. Private equity and private credit managers with heavy software exposure were pulled into the downdraft, sparking a liquidity crisis in some well-known private funds. Investors are split on the credibility of the fear: some warn AI adoption will accelerate and sustain the boom, while others argue it is illogical to assume AI will replace complex, mission-critical platforms. Many data vendors with huge installed bases claim AI can’t replicate proprietary feeds, even if the market is pricing risk well ahead of any proof.

As AI technology broadens from infrastructure to applications, the market has been excessively quick to crown AI “winners” and disruption “losers,” and the uncertainty around potential losers is difficult to disprove without clear signs of earnings acceleration—especially with tighter corporate spending and internal AI projects that can siphon IT budgets. Amid the volatility, a theme emerged around “HALO” stocks—companies with perceived “hard assets and low obsolescence.” Many such stocks were insulated from the sell-off, while other winners were closely tied to the infrastructure build-out being fueled by hyper-scaler capital expenditures (capex). Businesses within industrials that address data center power, cooling, and storage constraints saw impressive growth with a meaningful pipeline of future projects that has yet to be fully reflected in backlogs. While the market continues to sort through the implications of AI-driven disruption, volatility is likely to remain elevated until the fundamental winners and losers are fully clear—a process that could take years to play out.

Overall, U.S. equity markets finished lower for the quarter, closing a volatile start to the year amid ongoing uncertainty tied to the war in Iran and high volatility related to AI sentiment. Even as cyclical/value leadership faded later in the quarter, value stocks sharply outperformed growth, with the Russell 1000 Value Index returning 2.10% for the quarter versus a -9.78% return for the Russell 1000 Growth Index. Amid signs of market broadening, small caps outperformed large caps during the quarter, with the Russell 2000 Index finishing +0.89%. Small caps and value benchmarks benefited from the substantial performance of the energy sector, which rose 38% in the quarter.

Fund performance and attribution

Top Contributors And Detractors To Quarter-End Fund Performance

Contributors

  • Modine Manufacturing Co. (MOD)
  • Carpenter Technology Corp. (CRS)
  • Argan, Inc. (AGX)
  • DigitalOcean Holdings, Inc. (DOCN)
  • AAR Corp. (AIR)

Detractors

  • iRhythm Holdings, Inc. (IRTC)
  • Genius Sports Ltd. (GENI)
  • ADMA Biologics, Inc. (ADMA)
  • Commvault Systems, Inc. (CVLT)
  • RadNet, Inc. (RDNT)

The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Discussion Of Contributors

Modine Manufacturing (MOD) moves full speed into AI-driven cooling. Modine designs mission-critical heating, cooling, and ventilation systems, with a growing focus on its climate solutions segment serving data centers. Shares rallied during the quarter as Modine beat financial expectations, driven by surging data center demand, improved profitability, and higher guidance. Management also announced a strategic shift away from legacy auto components, repositioning the company as a pure-play climate solutions and data center provider. This captured the attention of investors who had otherwise avoided the company. With accelerating demand for data center services, we feel that Modine is well positioned for sustained growth and margin expansion.

Tailwinds carry Carpenter Technology Corp. (CRS) to new heights. Carpenter Technology manufactures high-performance alloy metals used in aerospace, defense, and power generation. After years of capacity constraints across the aerospace industry, Carpenter Technology is benefiting from increased production rates at Boeing and recent regulatory approvals that are driving demand for mission-critical materials and components. Producing advanced alloys is both complex and capital intensive, giving Carpenter robust pricing power, which drove outsized earnings growth during the quarter. Carpenter remains well positioned to deliver strong performance through pricing, mix, and sustained aerospace demand.

Argan, Inc. (AGX), relieves skilled labor bottlenecks. Power has become a critical input to the massive AI infrastructure build-out and the labor required to increase power output is in short supply. In addition to AI, increases in electric vehicle adoption, manufacturing, and automation have sparked high demand for energy consumption—a condition the U.S. labor force is not currently equipped to accommodate. As one of only a few contractors capable of building large-scale power plants, Argan benefits from pricing power and strong demand across both gas-fired and renewable projects tied to grid modernization. During the quarter, Argan shares rallied, driven by stronger-than-expected margins and higher capacity additions, which helped ease concerns around tight labor and supply constraints. With clear visibility from backlogs, Argan is positioned for attractive growth, driven by the critical need for increased power output.

Discussion Of Detractors

iRhythm Holdings, Inc. (IRTC), skipped a beat in the quarter. The company offers a next-generation medical device for patients with heart irregularities. The company's monitor, called Zio, digitally monitors a patient's heart rate for up to two weeks and continuously reports data back to a doctor. As a superior alternative to the current standard level of care, iRhythm's devices are more likely to identify heart issues before they cause serious problems for patients. Despite beating the market's growth expectations and providing positive revenue guidance for 2026, iRhythm's stock was caught up in "AI doomerism" during the quarter as investors raised questions on the strength of the company's moat. For the time being, we view the valuation pressure as largely sentiment driven and unrelated to underlying fundamentals. We continue to hold the position and will closely monitor the competitive landscape.

Investors called their bets off for Genius Sports Ltd. (GENI). Genius Sports is a leading business-to-business sports data and technology provider that supplies official real-time data, low-latency video, and betting technology. The company excels at integrity monitoring to major sports leagues, sportsbooks, and media companies. The company is positioned to benefit from continued U.S. sports betting expansion, margin improvement, and the evolution of its media technology offerings. Despite solid top-line growth, shares performed poorly during the quarter as profitability concerns and broader risk-off sentiment weighed on the stock. This was compounded by skepticism surrounding the company's $1.2 billion acquisition of Legend and thus widely viewed as high risk and poorly timed. In response to heightened near-term uncertainty and execution risk related to mergers and acquisitions, we trimmed the position and are closely monitoring the fundamental outlook.

ADMA Biologics, Inc., faced a clot in cash conversion. ADMA is a biopharmaceutical company focused on plasma-derived immunoglobulin therapies for patients with immune deficiencies and select infectious disease risks, including RSV. Its portfolio includes company-owned plasma collection centers that secure supply and enable end-to-end manufacturing control. During the quarter, elevated inventories and slower cash conversion temporarily pressured working capital and "days sales outstanding" despite continued healthy end-market demand. These dynamics were exacerbated by a short seller report that heightened concerns, although management emphasized the transitory nature of the issues and expects normalization ahead. We maintained our position, supported by expectations for continued treatment adoption and strong margin expansion.

Fund positioning and outlook

Fog of war

Moving through 2026, U.S. equity markets face heightened geopolitical risk, elevated macroeconomic uncertainty, and an increasingly volatile market structure. This marks a sharp change from the start of the year when investors anticipated stronger growth driven by rate cuts and fiscal stimulus. The war in Iran has clearly altered that outlook, creating uncertainty about whether current volatility will be brief, similar to "Liberation Day" in 2025, or more persistent. A prolonged energy disruption would likely pressure what has otherwise been a resilient economy. This foggy backdrop poses challenges, particularly in markets dominated by short-term trading activity.

A proven playbook

Over decades of managing growth equity portfolios, our team has navigated many periods of uncertainty. We have developed a disciplined playbook for these environments and are relying on that experience today.

While the Middle East conflict may drive volatility and shifts in leadership, we are not making material portfolio changes. The situation remains fluid, and if energy pressures ultimately force a resolution, as we believe is likely, the structural themes that previously drove U.S. equities should reassert themselves. Rather than reacting to headlines, we believe consistency and bottom-up stock selection are paramount. Macroeconomic events matter, but they do not change our focus on companies with durable fundamentals and superior earnings growth.

The tariff-driven volatility of 2025 offers a useful reminder. While some peers repositioned portfolios based on policy extrapolations, we effectively deployed our investment process and stayed focused on disciplined execution. In 2026, we will continue to make decisions one company at a time.

Scarcity of growth

We feel that slowing economic growth is likely to reemerge as a defining theme. The energy shock arrived as the economy was already struggling to gain momentum. Fragile consumer spending, tariff uncertainty, a challenged business climate, reduced immigration, government shutdowns, and mounting public debt all point to a lower-growth environment.

History suggests growth stocks tend to perform well during periods of sluggish expansion, which we believe may characterize 2026. Many portfolio holdings have demonstrated resilient growth through past downturns. Businesses with fundamental momentum, pricing power, and consistent cash flows often command a “scarcity premium” in uncertain markets—an environment that aligns well with our investment style.

Capitalizing on dislocations

U.S. equities have also experienced extreme speculation around AI, often driven by retail trading and momentum. Knee-jerk reactions have created “AI doomerism,” disconnecting stock prices from fundamentals. Recently, several portfolio companies saw valuation compression despite delivering strong results and positive estimate revisions. We believe this is an irrational dynamic that creates opportunity for long-term investors.

AI may prove to be one of the most significant computing shifts ever, but as with prior technological breakthroughs, identifying ultimate winners and losers will take years. The transition will bring competition, margin pressure, and disruption. Our task is to balance capitalizing on opportunities with humility that is grounded in deep research, patience when it’s warranted, and conservative assumptions. We believe our barbell portfolio approach is well suited to this environment, allowing us to manage risk while also capturing the market’s mispricing of exceptional growth businesses.

Broadening is underway

The first quarter reinforced a trend developing since late 2025: Market leadership is broadening. Beneath the surface, improving participation and sector rotation suggest a healthier market structure that is less reliant on mega-cap stocks. While early gains have favored lower-quality cyclical, we expect that leadership to fade. We are particularly optimistic about select mid- and small-cap growth companies, where valuations remain attractive and earnings growth is accelerating. We believe that our portfolios are well positioned to benefit from these trends down cap.

Closing thoughts

We are encouraged by the fundamental performance of many portfolio holdings, especially across AI infrastructure, health care, aerospace, e-commerce, and digital platforms. Our focus on resilient growth businesses on the “right side of change” remains central to our strategy. Despite elevated uncertainty, we are confident that this disciplined, time-tested approach should deliver strong long-term outcomes for our clients.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Russell 2000® Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 2000 companies with higher price/book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with lower price/book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000® Index, which represents approximately 8% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000 Index. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition Of Terms:

Active share of an account is calculated by subtracting a portfolio's overlap with its performance benchmark from 100%. The portfolio-level overlap compares parent-level weights between positions held in both the portfolio and benchmark, summing the lesser of such weights.

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

Long-term growth forecast is the annual earnings-per-share growth that the company can sustain over the next 3 or 5 years based on Allspring estimate data.

Price/earnings to growth ratio (PEG ratio) uses long-term earnings growth estimates. The long-term earnings growth is the annual earnings-per-share growth that the company can sustain over the next 3 or 5 years based on estimate data.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Smaller company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than those of larger companies. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.
